# Assignment 04 — Ike

**GitHub username: iykay-47**

**Date completed: 2026-06-02**

## 1. Answers to the 11 questions

**Q1 — default-bridge DNS gap:** ...
```
➜  ~ docker container run --rm -it --network container:api nicolaka/netshoot \
  sh -c 'echo "--- resolv.conf ---"; cat /etc/resolv.conf; \
         echo "--- dig db ---"; dig +short db; \
         echo "--- getent ---"; getent hosts db || echo "not found"'

# Generated by Docker Engine.
# This file can be edited; Docker Engine will not make further changes once it
# has been modified.

nameserver 192.168.1.254
search lan

# Based on host file: '/run/systemd/resolve/resolv.conf' (legacy)
# Overrides: []

--- dig db ---
--- getent ---
not found
```
From the above, the /etc/resolv.conf show pointing to the host external resolver. Even when set to 127.0.0.1, it will still return as empty, this is because doker expliitly disables internal service discovery and container name resolution on the default bridge network although present by default in user-defined bridge networks.
The result of that is the bridge network resolves dns using the host machine's resolver which has no record of other containers on the bridge network as it tries to externally resolve dns provided to it.

Docker embedded DNS resolver queries for external resolution on the default bridge network while it queries for internal resolution on a user-defined bridge network. 


**Q2 — db IP after restart + why hard-coding is wrong:**

```
➜  ~ docker container restart db
db
➜  ~ sleep 2
➜  ~ DB_IP_NEW=$(docker container inspect db -f '{{.NetworkSettings.Networks.bridge.IPAddress}}')
➜  ~ echo "DB_IP was $DB_IP, now $DB_IP_NEW"                                                     
DB_IP was 172.17.0.2, now 172.17.0.2
```

The IP of the container did not change. While docker tries to ensure containers retain their ips after a restart, it is worth noting docker still assigns ips to containers based on a first come first serve basis in a dynamic manner using the Dynamic Host Control Protocol (DHCP). As such a restart in a large amount of containers in a changed order might lead to a change in ip address of a container.

This gives rise to the problem with hard-coding ips in a dynamic config environment, which is the container might end up connecting to another container/database which may lead to corruption in data usage or app failure as the new ip might be pointing to a non-exixtent ip or a container without required config.

This is same problem as having no DNS, because there is a lack or auto ip-resolution.

The Only way I think this might work is using the side car method `--network container:<container_name>`. But not adviceable as it increases vulnerabilty and blast raidius in case of an attack as config is changed to resolve and serve on localhost between services.

**Q3 — subnet/gateway + flags to control them:** ...

```
➜  ~ docker network inspect cohort-net --format '{{json .IPAM.Config}}' | jq
[
  {
    "Subnet": "172.18.0.0/16",
    "Gateway": "172.18.0.1"
  }
]

➜  ~ docker network create --help | grep -E 'subnet|gateway'
      --gateway ipSlice       IPv4 or IPv6 Gateway for the master subnet (default [])
      --subnet strings        Subnet in CIDR format that represents a network segment

```
`Subnet: "172.18.0.0/16 "Gateway IP : "172.18.0.1"`
Was ramdonly associated by to the network by the docker. In order to intetnionally select/allocate the network resources such as subnet and gateway, the `--subnet` and `gateway` flag as used.

There are many more flags used to manually design the network during creation.



**Q4 — user-defined bridge DNS + design implication:** ...

```
➜  ~ docker container run --rm -it --network cohort-net nicolaka/netshoot \
  sh -c 'echo "--- dig db ---"; dig +short db; \
         echo "--- dig api ---"; dig +short api; \
         echo "--- curl api ---"; curl -sf http://api:8080/healthz && echo'
--- dig db ---
172.18.0.2
--- dig api ---
172.18.0.3
--- curl api ---
ok
```
The dig query here actually succeeds as it queries docker default DNS resolver at 127.0.0.11 which automatically adds and tracks all user-defined bridge networks and endpoints conneted to them as opposed to part1 where the DNS resolves to the host resolver.

In a 10-service stack, where each service needs to call/communicate witih atleast one other service, and containers as treated as catlles than pets. The ability to reach other contaniners via human readable names that are automatically resolved to IPs that might change constantly is a game changer that abstracts away tedious work of constantly tracking and assigning ips manually.

This also reduces the amount of future errors generated from the networking stack.

**Q5 — what isolated the stranger + how to bridge it:**

```
➜  ~ docker container run --rm --network cohort-other nicolaka/netshoot \
  sh -c 'echo "--- ping db ---"; ping -c 2 -W 1 db 2>&1; \
         echo "--- dig db ---"; dig +short db'
--- ping db ---
ping: db: Try again
--- dig db ---
```

Docker uses linux network primitive namespace and cgroups to control and isolate networks created from other networks. This is implemented via the Container Networking Model (CNM).

This prevents sandboxes without an endpoint in a particular network to from reaching or discovering other containers in a another network.

**Q6 — `-p HOST:CONTAINER` semantics:**
```
➜  ~ docker container run --rm --network cohort-net nicolaka/netshoot \
  curl -sf --max-time 3 http://api:18080/healthz \
  && echo " (port 18080 from container WORKED??)" \
  || echo " (port 18080 from container failed — as expected)"
 (port 18080 from container failed — as expected)
```

The third probe uses the netshoot networking container utility to probe port 18080 on thar api container. While this is resolved properly by docker internal DNS resolver, api is only serving/listening for traffic on port 8080 which is binded to the host port 18080 to allow external traffic.

As such trying to probe api on port 18080 which isn't configured/programmed to accept/listen for traffic, will fail unless it is explicitly configures to serve traffic on such port.

The `-p HOST:CONTAINER` simply executes Destination Network Address Translation (DNAT) from traffic external to the host to the container. This in simple terms transfers traffic requests and packets recieved by the host on a specified port to the specified container port. In essence binding the specified host port to the container's port. The host port cannot be attached/used unless unbinde or reassigned.

The container's listenning port does not change unless such setting is altered from within the container via app code or config.

FROM a linux machine `sudo iptables -t nat -L DOCKER -n 2>/dev/null` shows the DNAT config. What port on host accepts traffic and where it forwards such request to. In this case: accepting tcp requests from port 18080 and forwarding to <container_ip>:8080. 

**Q7 — `docker rm -v` vs named-volume behavior:**

```
➜  ~ docker container rm -fv throwaway-db 
throwaway-db
➜  ~ docker volume ls --filter name=cohort-db-data
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     cohort-db-data
```

the `-f` flag/option removes only anonymous volumes attached to a contianer. As such using this lets named volumes persist while any anonymous volume created by docker is deleted.

Anonymouch volumes may persist after container deletion but since it wasn't specified during creation, it endsup been forgotten and populating the disk. Named volumes require a `docker volume rm` command to be deleted

**Q8 — bind vs named volume + why not to poke the mountpoint:**
```
➜  ~ docker volume inspect cohort-demo --format '{{.Mountpoint}}'
/var/lib/docker/volumes/cohort-demo/_data
```

Question 8: name two things you can do with a bind mount that you can't (easily) do with a named volume, and two reasons you should still prefer a named volume for app data in production. The Mountpoint of a named volume is a host path — why is poking at it directly considered a bad idea?

Bind Mounts Pros:
- There is a Zero-copy dev loop, i.e instant changes are made. Once edited on host, container sees changes immediately.
- The host owns the data and as such survives container removal. Also host own permission settings on such files which makes it easy to inspect with regular tools.

Why Name Volumes should still be preferred:
- Docker manages lifecycle (creation, removal, driver). Also manages permissions when specified during creation hence there is no 2 way friction between host permisisonand container permissions for access to files.

- It is possiblle to use volume driver plugins to attach cloud/NFS storage. This is one of the biggest plus as there might be a limitiation to available system available memory for storage purpopses.

**Q9 — When tmpfs is the right choice:**

tmpfs is usually the right choice when passing decrypted secrets and cache not required to persist such as payment details and security vulnerable data.

**Q10 — Stopping db before backup + production alternative:**

```
➜  ~ docker container exec db-verify psql -U cohort -d cohort -c 'SELECT id, body FROM notes;'
 id |          body          
----+------------------------
  1 | this note IS PROTECTED
(1 row)
```

Stopping a primary db server which writes (write permission) prevents logical data corruption which arrises from mis-write of data into a disk. As such during back up partial data write and backup will be prevent and only complete data will backed up.

For a production-grade Postgres workload, the alternative to stop-and-backup will involve moving write permissinos to the read-replica while the primary is backed up.

**Q11 — compose-managed network/volume + external:true:**

```
➜  assignment-04 docker compose ps
NAME                  IMAGE                COMMAND                  SERVICE   CREATED          STATUS                    PORTS
assignment-04-api-1   cohort-api:0.1.0     "gunicorn --bind 0.0…"   api       50 seconds ago   Up 43 seconds (healthy)   0.0.0.0:18080->8080/tcp, [::]:18080->8080/tcp
assignment-04-db-1    postgres:16-alpine   "docker-entrypoint.s…"   db        50 seconds ago   Up 49 seconds (healthy)   5432/tcp.


➜  assignment-04 curl -s http://localhost:18080/notes
[{"body":"this note IS PROTECTED","id":1}]

➜  assignment-04 docker network ls
NETWORK ID     NAME                       DRIVER    SCOPE
2ba8dc640e70   assignment-04_cohort-net   bridge    local

```
Docker compose adds the name of the folder which the compose file exists as a prefix to resources which it creates. The "assignment-04-" was appended as a prefix to the container names and "assignment-04_" for network name. Pre-created resources can be specified using the `external: true` attribute, which means compose does not manage it.

Since `external: true` ensures the volume will be managed outside of docker compose. This means in the event of accidental detion of container and associated volumes created by compose or an outage where a container becomes unreacheable, the data stored in those volumes will persist especially if external (non-host) storage drivers are used. This means operations can be restored from same volume without waiting to re-establish connection with the faulty container or worse case loss of entire volume.

## 3. Files

### `api/Dockerfile`
```
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7

# ── build stage ──
FROM python:3.11-slim AS build
RUN python -m venv /opt/venv
ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# ── runtime stage ──
FROM python:3.11-slim AS runtime
COPY --from=build /opt/venv /opt/venv
ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH

RUN <<EOF 
groupadd appmgt
useradd -g appmgt appmgt
mkdir /app
chown -R appmgt:appmgt /app
EOF

WORKDIR /app

COPY app.py .
EXPOSE 8080

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8080/healthz')" || exit 1

USER appmgt

CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8080", "app:app"]
```

### `api/app.py`
```
import os, time
import psycopg2
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify

app = Flask(__name__)

DB_HOST = os.environ.get("DB_HOST", "db")
DB_USER = os.environ.get("DB_USER", "cohort")
DB_PASS = os.environ.get("DB_PASS", "cohort")
DB_NAME = os.environ.get("DB_NAME", "cohort")


def connect():
    for _ in range(30):
        try:
            return psycopg2.connect(
                host=DB_HOST, user=DB_USER, password=DB_PASS, dbname=DB_NAME
            )
        except psycopg2.OperationalError:
            time.sleep(1)
    raise RuntimeError("db never came up")


@app.before_request
def ensure_schema():
    if getattr(app, "_ready", False):
        return
    with connect() as c, c.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS notes (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, body TEXT NOT NULL)"
        )
        c.commit()
    app._ready = True


@app.get("/notes")
def list_notes():
    with connect() as c, c.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("SELECT id, body FROM notes ORDER BY id")
        return jsonify([{"id": i, "body": b} for i, b in cur.fetchall()])


@app.post("/notes")
def add_note():
    body = request.json.get("body", "")
    with connect() as c, c.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("INSERT INTO notes (body) VALUES (%s) RETURNING id", (body,))
        c.commit()
        return jsonify({"id": cur.fetchone()[0], "body": body}), 201


@app.get("/healthz")
def healthz():
    return ("ok", 200)
```

### `compose.yml`
```
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: cohort
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: cohort
      POSTGRES_DB: cohort
    volumes:
      - cohort-db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U cohort"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - cohort-net

  api:
    image: cohort-api:0.1.0
    environment:
      DB_HOST: db
    ports:
      - "18080:8080"
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - cohort-net

networks:
  cohort-net:

volumes:
  cohort-db-data:
    external: true
```

## 5. One trade-off I had to make

Using `-p` DNAT port mapping over host. While using `host` offers minimal lag when accessing data, it also goes againt the entire idea of why containers were created "isolation". Port mapping might increase lag time, it also gives the host fredon to map various services to various ports without exposing thos ports to the container. this reduces vulnerability layers for potential attacks.

Also Compose seems to be the better choice for more granular control of bulk docker resource creation, ease of resource creation and management and ease of editing commands without retyping entire commands and adhoc scripts.

## 6. One thing I'm still unsure about

If docker has a hard-coded protocal to designating subnets and ip address. And which layer of linux networking primitives docker uses to retain stopped container ips and the volume of container restarts it will require to see a change in ips.
